DEFENSE AGAINST FALSE BREAKOUTS — If in position during false breakout: 1) Reduce trading size. 2) Place wider stops. 3) Be ready to reposition if stopped. 4) Consider buying protective puts (long) or calls (short) during high volatility. 5) If stopped on false breakout, don't be shy about re-entering — professionals attempt multiple entries. Your small position size (2% rule) gives you the flexibility to do all this.

MAXIMUM POSITIONS — At 2% risk per trade and 6% monthly limit, maximum 3 simultaneous positions with full risk. If risk 1% per trade, can have 6 positions. More positions = more diversification but more complexity. Don't exceed your ability to monitor and manage. Each new position reduces available risk for remaining trades. Consider correlation: 3 correlated positions act as one big position for risk purposes.

Leveraged ETF strategy: 2x and 3x leveraged ETFs have structural decay from daily rebalancing. Short both bull and bear versions (e.g., short SPXL + short SPXS) to capture volatility decay. Works best in high-volatility, choppy markets. Requires careful rebalancing and borrow cost analysis.
The 90% rule: 90% of traders lose 90% of their money in 90 days. The difference between winners and losers is not knowledge but discipline and risk management. Protect capital first, profits second.

LIVERMORE'S RULE OF CONSERVATION: 'A man must believe in himself and his judgment if he expects to make a living at this game. But he must also conserve his capital.' Self-belief without capital preservation is worthless. Always maintain enough cash to take advantage of the next opportunity. Being fully invested means having no buying power when the real opportunity arrives.

CRYPTO EXCHANGE SELECTION: Centralized exchanges (Binance, Coinbase) offer high liquidity and fiat on/off ramps but carry custodial risk. DEXs (Uniswap, dYdX) offer self-custody but higher slippage and gas costs. CEX liquidity >> DEX liquidity for large orders. Use CEXs for execution, DEXs for permissionless access. Always check exchange proof-of-reserves for solvency risk.

CRYPTO WALLET SECURITY HIERARCHY: Hardware wallets (Ledger/Trezor) = most secure for long-term holds. Paper wallets = secure if generated offline properly. Desktop wallets = good security, full control. Mobile wallets = convenient for small spending amounts. Online/exchange wallets = highest risk, use only for active trading capital. Never store large amounts on exchanges.

LEARN FROM LOSSES: 'In losing money I have gained experience and accumulated a lot of valuable don'ts.' Livermore went broke multiple times but never made the same mistake twice. Keep a detailed loss journal. After every loss, write: what went wrong, why, and what you'll do differently. Losses are tuition. The uneducated trader pays tuition repeatedly for the same lesson.

LIVERMORE'S SECRET: His edge was not in predicting direction—it was in position management. He started small, added to winners, cut losers immediately, and was always ready to reverse if the market told him he was wrong. His system was: small initial risk, maximum reward on right trades, minimum loss on wrong trades. This asymmetry is the only reliable edge in trading.

DEV-STOP (Cynthia Kase) — Calculate ATR standard deviation over 30 days. Level 1 stop: ATR + 1σ + 10% correction. Level 2 stop: ATR + 2σ + 20% correction. Addresses non-normal distribution of prices (skewed right). Tighter than pure volatility stops but incorporates probability. 1σ encompasses ~67% of prices, 2σ ~97%. Use level 1 for aggressive systems, level 2 for conservative.

CRYPTO BUBBLE vs NETWORK EFFECT: Sharp crypto gains resemble tech bubbles but differ fundamentally—Bitcoin isn't a company that can lose profitability. The network effect means value grows as adoption increases. However, protocol-level failures CAN collapse price. Don't confuse network-effect growth with speculative bubbles, but don't ignore protocol risk either.

ENTRY EVALUATION — 95% of traders focus on finding great entries. Yet entry is LEAST important component. Few entries beat random significantly over 20-day holding period. Best entries: channel breakout >40 days, volatility breakout 0.8×ATR, large single-bar move. But even these only slightly improve reliability. Focus instead on: exits (determine R), position sizing (determines profit magnitude), and expectancy (the complete picture).

Forced asset flow momentum: Index rebalancing, margin calls, fund redemptions create persistent buying/selling pressure. Stocks added to S&P 500 outperform before inclusion. Leveraged ETF rebalancing creates predictable daily flow. Exploit by front-running known forced flows. Edge: structural, not behavioral.

Trailing one-bar entry strategy: After momentum and price/time targets align, enter on break of signal bar high (long) or low (short). Stop placed beyond opposite end of signal bar. Tight stop = favorable risk/reward. If stopped out, re-enter on next signal bar. Works on all timeframes from 5-min to monthly.

LIMIT MOVES IN FUTURES — Most futures have daily limits beyond which prices cannot move. String of limit days = terrifying, loser stuck unable to get out while account destroyed. Fears overdone: limit moves' heyday was 1970s inflation. Defense: 1) Don't over-leverage. 2) Diversify across uncorrelated markets. 3) Keep position sizes small enough to survive multiple limit moves. 4) Use options for catastrophic protection.

BEGINNER, SEMIPRO, PRO PROGRESSION — Beginner: small account, learning, paper trading, 0.5% risk per trade. Semipro: consistent profits, growing account, 1% risk, beginning to trade for income. Pro: full-time, multiple strategies, 1-2% risk, trading is primary income. Each level requires different position sizing, different markets, different expectations. Don't skip stages — the market punishes premature ambition.

Turtle entrepreneur traits: (1) Nonconformists—self-reliance. (2) Emotionally aloof—not cold, but can ignore noise. (3) Risk takers—comfortable with uncertainty. (4) Socially persuasive. (5) Autonomous. (6) Change seekers. (7) Energetic. These separated successful from failed Turtles. Trading rules alone are insufficient.

ATR calculation: True Range = max(High-Low, |High-PrevClose|, |Low-PrevClose|). ATR = N-period moving average of True Range. Use 20-period ATR for position sizing and stops. ATR measures volatility in absolute terms. Normalizing risk by ATR ensures equal risk per position across all markets.
Volatility regime adjustment: In high VIX (>25) environment, reduce position sizes by 50%, widen stops by 50%, and reduce number of open positions. Capital preservation in storms, aggressive in calm.
Never risk more than 2% of equity per trade. Maintain 2:1 reward-to-risk minimum. Never add to losing positions. Know your max acceptable drawdown before starting. Keep position sizes within reason.

Dollar Smile Theory: USD strengthens in two scenarios - 1) US economy outperforming (risk-on, USD as growth currency) and 2) Global risk aversion flight-to-safety. USD weakest in middle scenario - US economy weak but no global crisis.

Random entry test for exits: Use random coin-flip entries to isolate exit strategy performance. An exit that profits from random entries proves the exit itself has edge. SES profited from random entries on S&P 500. This proves exit strategy can be more important than entry.

John W. Henry principle: Human nature is trend following's edge. Most traders can't handle giving back profits after a trend ends, so they abandon systems during drawdowns. The few who endure reap outsized returns. Discipline > intelligence. 'If you don't feel pain during a drawdown, you're not trading big enough.'

Kalman filter for pairs trading: Dynamically estimates hedge ratio between two instruments, adapting to changing relationships. Superior to static regression hedge ratio. Filter automatically adjusts position sizes as relationship drifts. Also useful for detecting regime changes in cointegration relationship.

Limit-up rules trap traders in losing positions. Weinstein lost $600K+ over 5 consecutive limit-down days in soybeans unable to exit. Price limits protect the exchange, not the trader. Be aware of limit risk in futures.

Breakout model types tested: (1) Close-only channel breakout. (2) Highest high/lowest low breakout. (3) Volatility breakout. (4) Combined channel + volatility. Testing 36 markets over 15 years: All profitable. Close-only had fewest whipsaws. Volatility breakout had best risk-adjusted returns. Combined was most robust across market types.

A greedy trader always blows out. Kovner knew brilliant traders who knew markets better than he did, but he kept money and they didn't. The difference: position size. For every 1 contract Kovner traded, they traded 10.

Game theory for traders: Each trade is independent. Don't think about past wins/losses when sizing next trade. Don't vary position size based on confidence—confidence is unreliable. Use mechanical rules for EVERY decision. Take every signal, manage risk per trade, let edge play out over hundreds of trades.

Weinstein's devastating soybean loss: had a specific dollar target ($350K for a castle) that was driving position size, not market analysis. Never set specific dollar targets for trades. Let market conditions determine when to exit, not your spending desires.

Never average losers. 'Losers average losers'—Paul Tudor Jones. When trade goes against you, exit per system rules. Adding to losing position doubles risk on a trade already proven wrong. Trend following NEVER adds to losers—only to winners via pyramiding. This single rule prevents account destruction.

Never have more than 5% in one idea. Related positions count as one idea. Two grain markets long = one idea. The purpose is to survive 20+ consecutive wrong trades. Even great traders have extended losing streaks.

DOLLAR STOP LIMITATIONS — Dollar stops: psychologically easy (decide how much you'll lose), not predictable by others, good when beyond MAE. BUT: people confuse dollar stops with position sizing and ignore the 'how much' question. Setting $1,000 stop on $100K account = 1% risk — that's position sizing, not stop placement. Your stop should be based on MARKET STRUCTURE (volatility, S/R), not on how much you want to lose.

Kovner risks no more than 1% of portfolio on any single trade. He studies correlation of trades daily to reduce exposure. Eight highly correlated positions = one position eight times as large. A mistake in position correlation is root of serious problems.

Holding winners is equally important as cutting losers. If you don't stay with your winners, you cannot pay for the losers. Marcus's most devastating experience was a PROFITABLE trade where he got out prematurely — soybeans went limit-up 12 more days.

Manual backtesting > automated: Manual testing builds expertise and pattern recognition. Keep sessions under 2 hours. Test at least 200 trades before going live. Track win rate, avg win/loss, max drawdown.

Equalization of dollar volatility: Before testing across instruments, normalize positions so equal dollar volatility per market. Use ATR: Position size = $Risk / (ATR x point value). Without normalization, one volatile market dominates portfolio performance. Essential for multi-market systems.

Place stops beyond technical barriers, not within trading ranges. Allocate max dollar risk to fewer contracts with wider stops — the REVERSE of typical trader who limits loss per contract but trades maximum contracts. Stop should indicate trade is wrong, not just max loss per contract.

BREAK-EVEN STOP: When It Helps vs Hurts: Moving stop to break-even after TP1 hit is STANDARD PRACTICE but has nuance. Helps: In trending markets, protects against reversal after partial profit. Prevents winning trades from becoming losers. Hurts: In volatile markets, break-even stops get wicked out before price continues in your direction. If your win rate is high (>60%), break-even stops reduce EV because you're converting winners into scratch trades. Rule: Use break-even in trending regimes (ADX > 25). Skip break-even in ranging regimes. Always move to break-even AFTER first scale-out, not before.

Risk of ruin for trend followers: Random effects dominate short-term results. Out of 100 random traders with slight edge, 17 had better 3.5yr MAR than best real system. Over 10+ years, random luck washes out. Minimum 5-year track record needed to distinguish skill from luck. Invest in people, not past performance.

SNOW FIGHT METAPHOR — Each trade = snowball thrown at wall. White snow = profits, black snow = losses. Net effect on wall = your equity change. THREE variables: 1) Reliability (probability of white vs black). 2) Reward-to-risk (size of white vs black). 3) Opportunity (how many snowballs = frequency). Multiply all three = expectancy. Position sizing = how many walls you're building simultaneously. All three variables matter, not just reliability.
Trading system is less important than beliefs about risk. System accounts for 10% of success. Psychology and risk management account for 90%. Simple systems are robust; complex systems break.
Regime detection tools: ADX>25 = trending (use trend strategies). ADX<25 = ranging (use range strategies). Bollinger bandwidth for volatility regime. Correlation stability for risk regime.

Managed futures as portfolio insurance: Allocating 20% to trend following improved traditional 60/40 portfolio returns while REDUCING max drawdown. Near-zero long-term correlation to stocks. During stock crashes (2000-02, 2008), managed futures were positive. The only strategy that's truly crisis-positive.

Combined stock + commodity portfolio: Stock scalping (short-term, mean reversion) + commodity trend following (medium-term, momentum). Correlation ~0.15. Combined: 23-37% annual, 6-13% max DD. Diversification across strategy types reduces risk more than diversification across markets within same strategy.

Look for hardiest method, not optimum method. Anyone can devise perfect system for the past. We don't trade markets, we trade money. Gold and cocoa are both 1% bets — they are the same. Risk is risk regardless of the instrument.

BACKTESTING PROTOCOL — Minimum 2 years historical data. Split into development (first 60%) and validation (last 40%). Walk forward one day at a time — never peek ahead. Record each signal, entry, exit, P&L. After development phase: test on out-of-sample data. If performance degrades significantly, system is curve-fit. Track: win rate, avg winner/loser, max consecutive losses, max drawdown, profit factor, expectancy per dollar risked.

Never commit more than 5% of your total capital to a single trade idea. If you take positions in two related grain markets, that is still ONE idea. This rule survived Marcus blowing up twice by betting everything on one trade.
Daily loss limit: If you lose 3% of account in a single day, stop trading for the day. If you lose 5% in a week, stop for the weekend. Prevents emotional spiral of revenge trading.
Risk 1-2% per trade maximum. Place stop beyond the zone (not at arbitrary pip distance). If zone is too wide for acceptable risk, skip the trade. Never widen a stop after entry.

MEAN REVERSION CONCEPT — Core: prices revert to average over time. Buy oversold, sell overbought. Works best in ranging markets. Strategies: Bollinger Band bounces, RSI extremes (<30 or >70), support/resistance bounces, pairs trading. Risk: catching falling knives in strong trends. Requires strict stops. Opposite of trend following. Identify ranging market first (ADX <20, clear S/R levels) before applying mean reversion.

PAYOUT VALIDATION AND WITHDRAWAL RULES: After passing evaluation: 1) Some firms require a 'verification' phase (second evaluation at reduced targets). 2) Payout splits typically 80-90% of profits. 3) Minimum profit threshold before withdrawal ($100-500). 4) Payout frequency (weekly, bi-weekly, monthly). 5) Some firms take fees from payouts. CRITICAL: Read the payout terms BEFORE paying evaluation fees. Some firms have hidden rules like 'first $X of profit goes to fees' or 'payouts only after Y trading days in funded phase'. Prefer firms with transparent, frequent payouts and no hidden deductions.

PTJ limits monthly losses: 3% stop on equity for the rest of the month if down. Never have a double-digit monthly loss. Reduce position size when trading poorly — trade SMALLEST position when trading is WORST.
Scaling out: Exit 1/3 position at 1x risk, 1/3 at 2x risk, final 1/3 with trailing stop. This locks in profits while allowing runners. Reduces emotional pressure of all-or-nothing exits.

When trading size, get out when the MARKET lets you out, not when you want to get out. To move a large position, don't wait until market is at new high/low where little volume trades. Liquidate half before the stop cluster zone, half beyond it.
Volatility-based position sizing: Use ATR(14) to normalize position sizes across pairs. Higher ATR = smaller position, lower ATR = larger position. This equalizes risk contribution from each trade.

Hite's friend lost everything on one gold trade — took sell signal, didn't follow it, went long, refused to exit. Lost the mansion. If you follow the system and take the sell signal, you make a fortune instead. System discipline is survival.

Don't ever average losers. Decrease trading volume when trading poorly; increase when trading well. Never trade in situations where you don't have control — don't risk significant money in front of key reports, that is gambling not trading.

If you can't take a small loss, sooner or later you will take the mother of all losses. Hussein's invasion of Kuwait parallels a trader holding a losing position — each day's procrastination resulted in more destruction. Cut losses early.
Position sizing formula: Position size = (Account Risk $) / (Stop Loss pips x pip value). Always calculate before entry. Never exceed 2% account risk. Adjust size inversely with stop distance.

OPTION DELTA FOR DIRECTIONAL PLAYS — When buying options, use delta 0.70 or better for directional plays. Higher delta = option moves more like stock. Buy in-the-money options with at least 2 weeks before expiration. If uncertain, go out to next month — it's like insurance. Use squeeze signals on underlying for timing. Options limit risk to premium paid while providing leverage on directional moves.

Don't trade when you can't afford to lose. Trading with scared money guarantees failure: you miss best opportunities (most risky), jump out of good positions prematurely, take profits too fast, and stay with losers from fear/indecision.

Market regime identification matrix: Trending+LowVol = trend continuation (best conditions). Trending+HighVol = volatile trend (reduce size). Ranging+LowVol = compression (prepare for breakout). Ranging+HighVol = choppy (avoid trading).

Don't trade on someone else's opinion. When you get in on another's analysis, you have no basis for deciding when to get out. You surrender the decision process, eliminating risk control. The market punishes all trading transgressions.

TREND FOLLOWING CONCEPT — Core: capture large moves in trending markets. Accept many small losses for occasional large wins. Let winners run, cut losers short. Don't predict, react. Enter on breakouts or pullbacks to MAs. Use trailing stops to exit. Never limit profits with fixed targets. Position size smaller (1% risk) for more volatility. Best markets: commodities, currencies, stock indices. Works because markets trend ~30% of time.

Flat time metric: Longest period between new equity highs. Top CTAs: 12-18 months flat time. If backtest shows 24+ months, system may be untradeable psychologically. Solution: Combine with uncorrelated strategy to reduce flat time. Two systems with 12-month flat individually may have 6-month combined.
Eckhardt: Never risk more than 2% on a single trade. Trading size at high end of linear performance range. Beyond optimum, performance plummets — even modestly too large is negative.

Always use protective stops. Actually place them in the market — a mental stop is not enough for most traders. The stop commits you to exit at a predetermined point. Never enter a position without knowing your exit point first.

Risk indicators for strategy monitoring: Track daily Sharpe ratio (rolling 6-month). If SR drops below 0.5 for 3+ months, investigate. Track hit rate, average R-multiple. If any metric deviates >2 sigma from expected, reduce position size or halt trading. Don't wait for drawdown to diagnose problems.

INSTITUTIONAL vs PRIVATE TRADER — Institutional traders have managers enforcing discipline: max risk per trade + max monthly drawdown. When they go independent, they lose without external discipline. Private traders need their OWN equivalent: 2% Rule (replaces per-trade limit) + 6% Rule (replaces monthly limit). Social pressure from peers in trading room = additional motivation. Create your own external accountability structures.

Always liquidate half position below new highs/lows, remaining half beyond that point. Floor traders buy to trigger stops at obvious levels then liquidate into them. Don't be the one getting stopped at the exact high/low.

Never hold a position that gaps sharply against you right after entry. A gap against you means something has changed. Get out. You can always reassess and reenter. The gap is a signal the trade premise may be invalid.

Carry trade: Borrow low-yield currencies (JPY, CHF) to buy high-yielders (AUD, NZD). Works in stable/trending markets. Unwinds violently during risk aversion. AUD/JPY is the classic carry pair - tracks risk sentiment.

KAUFMAN'S DRAWDOWN PROBABILITIES — With optimized position sizing: 16% chance of 10% drawdown. 2.5% chance of 20% drawdown. 0.5% chance of 30% drawdown. If you'd be in serious trouble at 20% loss, trade only a portion of allocated capital. Use these probabilities to size your overall exposure. Your psychology determines how much drawdown you can tolerate — design position sizing to match your pain threshold.

Broker selection criteria: Regulated (NFA/FCA), competitive spreads, reliable execution, negative balance protection, segregated client funds. Avoid unregulated offshore brokers despite higher leverage offered.

Create scenarios for each market at least weekly. Define expected ranges and what to do if price breaks out of those ranges. Have predetermined action plans — don't make decisions in the heat of the moment.

Equity-FX link: Rising US equities + rising USD = risk-on, US outperformance. Rising equities + falling USD = global risk-on, USD funding currency. Falling equities + rising USD = risk-off flight to safety.

PERCENT RETRACEMENT STOPS — Set stop at X% below entry price (common: 7-10% for stocks). Problem: $10 stock with 10% stop = $1 stop. $100 stock with 10% stop = $10 stop. Same percentage, wildly different dollar risk and R-multiples. Use only if based on MAE analysis showing actual retracement patterns. Better: volatility-based (ATR) stops adapt to each instrument's noise level. Never use arbitrary percentage without statistical backing.
If you don't bet, you can't win. If you lose all your chips, you can't bet. These two rules encompass all of risk management and opportunity management. Never forget both sides of this equation.
Eckhardt: Position sizing is more important than entry signals. The same entry signals with different position sizing methods produce wildly different results. Focus on HOW MUCH, not just WHEN.
Scaling into trends: Enter 1/3 position on breakout, add 1/3 on pullback to 20 EMA, add final 1/3 on continuation above breakout high. Trail stop to breakeven after first add.
Fear and greed cycle: Fear causes early exit of winners. Greed causes holding losers too long. The solution is mechanical rules: fixed stops, predetermined targets, position sizing formula.

Momentum strategy regime awareness: Momentum performed poorly 2007-2012 (mean-reverting regime). Before 2007 and after 2012, momentum was strong. Don't abandon a working strategy during temporary drawdown—but do reduce allocation during regime shifts. Monitor rolling Sharpe to detect regime changes early.

PYRAMIDING — Add to winning positions ONLY. Each new position smaller than previous. Move stops to breakeven on earlier positions before adding. Maintain overall risk within 2% of account per trade, 6% total. Use trailing stops based on SafeZone indicator. Pyramid when Impulse System confirms on each new timeframe. Never add to losing positions — this is the hallmark of amateur destruction.
Common mistakes: Overtrading, no stop losses, risking too much, revenge trading, moving stops, ignoring the trend. Every one of these is a discipline problem, not a knowledge problem.

ONE UNIT PER FIXED AMOUNT — Model 1: Buy 1 contract per $X equity. Example: 1 contract per $100K. Limitations: 1) Not all instruments are alike (corn vs S&P). 2) Can't increase exposure rapidly. 3) Always takes position even in poor opportunities. 4) Treats all investments equally. Worst model but most common among beginners. Should be replaced with percent risk or percent volatility model for professional results.

Composite sentiment score: COT extreme (weight 30%) + SSI extreme (25%) + options risk reversal (20%) + news headline extreme (15%) + Google Trends peak (10%). Score >80 = high conviction contrarian signal.
Scaling out: At 1:1 R:R, close 50% and move stop to breakeven. At 2:1, close 25% and trail remaining 25%. This guarantees profit while allowing the trade to run.

Entry techniques tested: (1) Channel breakout. (2) Moving average crossover. (3) Momentum threshold. (4) Volatility breakout. Testing across 30+ futures showed channel breakouts with ATR filter had best risk-adjusted performance for trend following.
Trading plan must be written and followed. Entry rules, exit rules, risk rules, and daily routine all documented. Review weekly. Deviation from plan = gambling, not trading.
Risk-on/Risk-off (RORO) framework: In risk-on, buy AUD/JPY, NZD/JPY, sell USD/CHF. In risk-off, buy USD/JPY (sometimes), CHF/JPY, sell AUD/USD. VIX is the RORO thermometer.
Trading is a business, not gambling. Create a business plan with: strategy rules, risk parameters, daily routine, profit targets, maximum drawdown limits. Review monthly.
Start with one pair only. Learn its personality, average range, key levels, and behavior during different sessions. Specialization beats diversification for beginners.
Risk management for trends: Start with 0.5% risk per trade. Add to winners up to 1% total risk on best setups. Trail stop to breakeven at 1x risk. Move stop to lock in 1x risk profit at 2x target.
Never move stop loss further from entry. Only move it to lock in profits (trailing stop). Moving stops wider is how small losses become account-destroying losses.
Multiple positions approach: Enter 2-3 positions. Close first at 1:1 R:R (covers risk), close second at 2:1, trail third position. This ensures profit on most trades while allowing runners.

DISCRETIONARY vs SYSTEM TRADING — System traders achieve more consistent results but best traders use discretionary. Professional system trader monitors system like a hawk, knows difference between normal drawdown and system deterioration. Automatic systems don't work because market follows imperfect laws of mass psychology, not physics. Use backtested components but retain judgment for combining them. All mechanical systems eventually self-destruct.
Seidler: Cannot win at leverage that makes you fearful. Reduce position until you can think clearly and objectively. Fear distorts judgment.

TRADER'S SPREADSHEET — Monthly tracking: opening equity, deposits/withdrawals, closing equity, monthly % gain/loss, YTD return. Per-trade: date, market, direction, entry, stop, target, exit, profit/loss, R-multiple, commission, net. Calculate: win rate, average winner/loser, largest winner/loser, max drawdown, expectancy per trade, profit factor. Plot equity curve monthly. If curve rises left to right, system works.

TRADING FOR FREEDOM — You can be free: live and work anywhere, be independent from routine, answer to nobody. But freedom requires innate traits: discipline, risk tolerance, facility with numbers. Discipline = choosing what you want MOST over what you want NOW. Without discipline, no system works. With discipline, even a mediocre system produces profits. Freedom is earned through thousands of disciplined decisions.

Oct 1987: market gapped past stop levels by 240 points vs normal 40-50 point slippage. When market is that out of line, get out ASAP. If you have any doubt about getting out fast in such a situation, you are in big trouble. Dennis.

Commodity trend system filter: ADX(14) > 25 AND price above/below 50-day MA AND volume > 20-day average. Triple filter reduces false breakouts by 50%. Trade count drops from ~200/yr to ~80/yr, but win rate improves from 38% to 48% and average win increases. Better risk-adjusted returns with fewer trades.

BENCHMARK COMPARISON — Always compare system returns to a benchmark: risk-free rate + equity risk premium. If your system returns 15% but benchmark returns 12%, your edge is only 3%. Factor in: time spent, stress, transaction costs, drawdowns. A 15% return with 20% max drawdown may not justify the effort vs passive indexing at 12%. Calculate risk-adjusted return: Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, SQN for apples-to-apples comparison.

Dennis's screening test: 63 true/false questions + 11 short answers. Selected people who could think independently, were comfortable with risk, had intellectual curiosity, and could handle losing. NOT selected: people who needed certainty or were emotionally reactive. Trading success starts with personality.

Currency correlations change over time. EUR/USD and USD/CHF have -0.94 monthly correlation. USD/JPY and USD/CHF have +0.83. Taking opposite positions on correlated pairs doubles exposure without realizing it.

Moving average trend-following model comparison: Simple MA crossover profitable but slow. EMA crossover faster but more whipsaws. Adaptive MA (Kaufman) best of all—adjusts smoothing to market efficiency. All MA systems have drawdown during choppy markets. Use trend filter (ADX > 25) to reduce false signals in ranging conditions.

Minervini: Position sizing based on volatility. More volatile stocks get smaller positions. If a stock moves 5% daily, position should be half the size of one that moves 2.5% daily. Normalize risk by volatility across all positions.

Managed futures allocation: Trend following has near-zero correlation to stocks/bonds over full market cycle. Adding 20% managed futures to traditional portfolio improves risk-adjusted returns. Biggest benefit comes during stock market crashes (crisis alpha). Correlation to S&P is slightly negative during bear markets.

Futures intermarket spread trading: Trade spread between related futures (crack spread = heating oil + gasoline - crude oil). Use cointegration to verify relationship. Enter when spread deviates >2 sigma from mean. Requires settlement prices from same time. Different closing times between exchanges need intraday data.

You can risk 1% of capital, 5%, or 10% — but realize the more you risk, the more volatile the results. The relationship between risk percentage and drawdown volatility is nonlinear. Seykota's key insight on position sizing.

CONFLUENCE ZONES — Strongest signals occur when multiple factors align: 1) Support/resistance level. 2) Channel line. 3) Moving average. 4) Fibonacci retracement. 5) Psychological round number. 6) Prior swing point. The more factors converging at one price level, the more significant that level. Trade with maximum position size at high-confluence zones, reduced size at isolated levels.

VX futures strategy: VIX futures are almost always in contango, creating predictable roll yield. Short VX front month, roll before expiration. During volatility spikes (contango inverts), go long. Simple but lucrative strategy requiring careful risk management—VX can spike 300%+ in days during crises.

Time-based exits: Exit after N bars regardless of profit/loss. Surprisingly effective—Donchian trend system with 80-day time exit outperformed breakout exit in tests. Time exit works because it captures trending portion and avoids prolonged consolidation. Best combined with minimum profit threshold.

DAY-TRADING RULES — Use 30-min and 5-min charts (Factor of Five). Weekly chart is essentially irrelevant for day-trading. Focus on highest-volume hours: first and last hour of regular session. Don't chase — if you miss an entry, wait for next setup. Set maximum daily loss limit (1-2% of account). Stop after 3 consecutive losses. Always use stop losses — no exceptions. Take profits at channel lines on intraday charts.

THE MARATHON METAPHOR: Trend following is not a sprint—it's a marathon. Drawdowns can last weeks or months. You'll feel like 'a punching bag who takes all the hits.' The key: stand up every time you get knocked down. Confidence comes from knowing that standing up again is the right thing to do. Track your equity curve, not individual trades.

Carry trade unwinding: When VIX spikes above 25, carry trades unwind rapidly. High-yielders fall, low-yielders rise. This creates 500-1000 pip moves in days. Risk management must account for this tail risk.
Pyramid into winners: Start with half position. Add quarter position at 1x risk profit. Add another quarter at 2x risk profit. Stop all at 1x risk below current price. Never pyramid into losers.
Tail risk hedging: Buy far OTM options on carry positions. Cost is minimal (0.5-1% annually) but provides catastrophic protection. Like insurance - seems wasteful until you need it.

Trading maxims backed by testing: 'Cut losses short' = TRUE, systems with tighter stops have better risk-adjusted returns. 'Let winners run' = TRUE, trailing stops outperform fixed targets. 'Always use stops' = SURPRISINGLY FALSE for some trend systems—no-stop versions of MA systems outperformed all stop variants.

Stock scalping system: Entry when price drops to support with oversold RSI (<20) and bounces. Stop: Below support by 1 ATR. Target: Next resistance or 2xATR. Time limit: Exit by close if target not reached. Win rate: 65%+. Profit/loss ratio: 1.5:1. High frequency, small profits, tight risk control.

Dennis's teaching was 'beyond my wildest imagination.' Most profitable trades required sitting through massive drawdowns. Turtles who couldn't handle giving back open profits failed. Key: Separate P&L from process. Check results weekly at most. Daily P&L watching destroys discipline and causes premature exits.

Zero-sum nature of markets: For every winner there's a loser. Trend followers profit because most participants are wrong most of the time (they buy tops, sell bottoms). The edge IS human behavioral bias—it's permanent. Trend following extracts money from emotional traders who can't cut losses or ride winners.

Time series vs cross-sectional momentum: Time series: Buy/sell based on own past return. Cross-sectional: Rank all instruments, buy winners, sell losers. Time series has higher Sharpe but bigger drawdowns. Cross-sectional is market-neutral but smaller returns. Combine both for all-weather momentum portfolio.

TIME STOPS — If position doesn't move in your favor within X periods, exit. Rationale: if market hasn't responded to your signal, the edge may be gone. Reduces opportunity cost of dead money. Typical: 5-10 bars for swing trades, 20-50 bars for position trades. Combine with trailing stop: time stop activates only if position is at or below entry after X periods. Don't use alone — pair with volatility or structural stop.

Options-implied volatility as risk gauge: When 1-month implied vol > 3-month (inverted term structure), market expects imminent crisis. Reduce exposure. Normal term structure (upward sloping) = stable conditions.

HEDGING TRAPPED POSITIONS — If platform goes down with open YM position: 1) Take opposite position in ES to hedge (long 2 YM = sell 2 ES). 2) Or place ES sell stop at price equivalent to YM stop level. This protects if market hits stop while you can't access YM. If market moves your way, you still participate. Always have backup plan for technology failure. Never have open positions without a hedge capability.

Straddle strategy for news: Place buy stop 30 pips above current price and sell stop 30 pips below. Use OCO (one-cancels-other) orders. One triggers on news spike. Cancel other. Risk: whipsaw both stops.

Carry trade management: Collect interest daily but monitor risk sentiment closely. Exit immediately when VIX spikes above 25 or equity markets drop >2% in a day. Carry can't compensate for capital loss.
Trend trading with multiple entries: Enter first position on trend line break. Add on pullback to 20 EMA. Add on break of swing high. Trail stop to each new swing low. Never risk more than 6% total.

Walton: If you can't give trading 100% energy and enthusiasm, don't trade. Walk away. He returned all investor money and stopped trading completely during a divorce. The discipline to NOT trade is as important as the discipline to trade.
